texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Diffusion and Permeation of Oxygen, Nitrogen, Carbon Dioxide, and Nitrogen Dioxide through Polytetrafluoroethylene
texts.text: Diffusion and Permeation of Oxygen, Nitrogen, Carbon Dioxide, and Nitrogen Dioxide through Polytetrafluoroethylene

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: R. A. Pasternak,  . V. Christensen, and J. Heller
texts.text: R. A. Pasternak,  . V. Christensen, and J. Heller

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymer Chemistry Department, Stanford Research Institute, Menlo Park, California 94025. Received December 19, 1969
texts.text: Polymer Chemistry Department, Stanford Research Institute, Menlo Park, California 94025. Received December 19, 1969

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ABSTRACT: The permeation of C02, 02, N2, and N204-N02 equilibrium mixtures through polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) has been investigated systematically. PTFE membranes between 1.5 and 5.7 mil thick were studied over a temperature range of 30-116=. For all the gases, the permeation and diffusion coefficients were found to be independent of membrane thickness, within the precision of the measurements. Linear Arrhenius plots were obtained for C02, 02, and N2. For the N204-N02 system, the temperature dependence of both permeation and diffusion was complex; the permeation data could be explained quantitatively in terms of the N204-N02 equilibrium. The parameters describing the diffusion and solution process indicate that the C02, 02, N2, and N204 molecules do not significantly interact with PTFE but move through preexisting channels and voids, whereas NO_> interacts strongly, though reversibly, with the polymer.
texts.text: ABSTRACT: The permeation of C02, 02, N2, and N204-N02 equilibrium mixtures through polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) has been investigated systematically. PTFE membranes between 1.5 and 5.7 mil thick were studied over a temperature range of 30-116=. For all the gases, the permeation and diffusion coefficients were found to be independent of membrane thickness, within the precision of the measurements. Linear Arrhenius plots were obtained for C02, 02, and N2. For the N204-N02 system, the temperature dependence of both permeation and diffusion was complex; the permeation data could be explained quantitatively in terms of the N204-N02 equilibrium. The parameters describing the diffusion and solution process indicate that the C02, 02, N2, and N204 molecules do not significantly interact with PTFE but move through preexisting channels and voids, whereas NO_> interacts strongly, though reversibly, with the polymer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The development of advanced liquid propulsion systems requires the use of expulsion bladders for storable propellants in the space environment. The bladder material must not only be chemically inert toward strong oxidants such as nitrogen dioxide but must also have a very low permeability toward these agents. Because of its chemical resistance and good mechanical properties, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) has received serious consideration as a bladder material and several space vehicles have been launched using PTFE expulsion bladders.
texts.text: The development of advanced liquid propulsion systems requires the use of expulsion bladders for storable propellants in the space environment. The bladder material must not only be chemically inert toward strong oxidants such as nitrogen dioxide but must also have a very low permeability toward these agents. Because of its chemical resistance and good mechanical properties, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) has received serious consideration as a bladder material and several space vehicles have been launched using PTFE expulsion bladders.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, permeation of oxidants through PTFE, while not detrimental in short missions, is of serious concern in long missions. Since only very limited data for the diffusion and permeation of noncondensable gases and none for nitrogen dioxide through PTFE are found in the literature,1·2 a systematic study of these gases was undertaken in an attempt to understand the details of the interaction between permeants and PTFE.
texts.text: However, permeation of oxidants through PTFE, while not detrimental in short missions, is of serious concern in long missions. Since only very limited data for the diffusion and permeation of noncondensable gases and none for nitrogen dioxide through PTFE are found in the literature,1·2 a systematic study of these gases was undertaken in an attempt to understand the details of the interaction between permeants and PTFE.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Experimental Section
texts.text: Experimental Section

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dynamic method for measuring permeation and diffusion rates of gases and vapors in polymer membranes has been described in detail elsewhere3 and is here only summarized. The two compartments of the permeation cell, which are separated by the test membrane, are open to the atmosphere. The permeant, gas or vapor, is admitted to one compartment replacing a carrier gas, usually helium. A second carrier gas stream flows at constant rate through the other compartment and sweeps the permeant, which diffuses through the membrane, to the thermal conductivity detector. The detector signal, which is originally zeroed with the carrier gas in both compartments, is at any instant proportional to the permeation rate. When the permeant is again replaced by the carrier gas, the membrane is degassed, and the signal returns to the base line. Thus, repeat measurements can be made with the same membrane over a wide temperature range and for diverse permeants
texts.text: The dynamic method for measuring permeation and diffusion rates of gases and vapors in polymer membranes has been described in detail elsewhere3 and is here only summarized. The two compartments of the permeation cell, which are separated by the test membrane, are open to the atmosphere. The permeant, gas or vapor, is admitted to one compartment replacing a carrier gas, usually helium. A second carrier gas stream flows at constant rate through the other compartment and sweeps the permeant, which diffuses through the membrane, to the thermal conductivity detector. The detector signal, which is originally zeroed with the carrier gas in both compartments, is at any instant proportional to the permeation rate. When the permeant is again replaced by the carrier gas, the membrane is degassed, and the signal returns to the base line. Thus, repeat measurements can be made with the same membrane over a wide temperature range and for diverse permeants

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The instrument used in this study is a prototype of the polymer permeation analyzer (PPA) (Dohrmann Instrument Co. (Infotronics), Mt. View). The cell and the plumbing were stainless steel; the effective membrane area was 3.9 cm2. The detector was a Loenco thermal conductivity cell (Dohrman Instrument Co.) which was run at 140 mA and thermostated at 80c. Its sensitivity for C02. 144 ppm/mV, did not change significantly with time, even after exposure to N02. The combination of the detector with a Sargent recorder (full scale deflection of 250 mm at an input of 0.4 mV) resulted in a detection limit of 0.2 ppm of C02 in helium. Helium was used as carrier gas for the study of all permeants, except of hydrogen, for which nitrogen was used. (With Ns as carrier gas the detector current was 50 mA.) The flow rates of the carrier gas were set between 0.5 and 1.5 ml/sec, the higher values being employed at high diffusion rates. At steady-state permeation, strict inverse proportionality between signal and flow rate was found as expected for a detector with linear response.
texts.text: The instrument used in this study is a prototype of the polymer permeation analyzer (PPA) (Dohrmann Instrument Co. (Infotronics), Mt. View). The cell and the plumbing were stainless steel; the effective membrane area was 3.9 cm2. The detector was a Loenco thermal conductivity cell (Dohrman Instrument Co.) which was run at 140 mA and thermostated at 80c. Its sensitivity for C02. 144 ppm/mV, did not change significantly with time, even after exposure to N02. The combination of the detector with a Sargent recorder (full scale deflection of 250 mm at an input of 0.4 mV) resulted in a detection limit of 0.2 ppm of C02 in helium. Helium was used as carrier gas for the study of all permeants, except of hydrogen, for which nitrogen was used. (With Ns as carrier gas the detector current was 50 mA.) The flow rates of the carrier gas were set between 0.5 and 1.5 ml/sec, the higher values being employed at high diffusion rates. At steady-state permeation, strict inverse proportionality between signal and flow rate was found as expected for a detector with linear response.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The detector sensitivity, which is a function of the gas or vapor sensed, was determined for C02 and H20. Helium with 0.21 vol % C02 and nitrogen with 0.20 vol % H2, were used as test gases. Moreover, the permeation rates of C02, 02, and N2 through a PTFE membrane were measured in a Barrer-type permeation cell (Linde Model CS 135). and compared with the equivalent signals obtained in the PPA. The permeation coefficients for C02 derived by the two independent approaches differed by 7%; the data reported here are based on the calibration of the PPA. The mole response factors of the detector for 02 and N2 (relative to C02) were found to be 1.20 and 1.22; the factor for N02 was assumed to be unity because its molecular weight differs by only two units from that of C02. No N204 reached the detector since in the helium stream the highest partial pressure of the nitrogen oxides, expressed as N02, was less than 0.1 %. Finally, the equilibrium solubility of N204-N02 in PTFE at a total pressure of 1 atm was measured over a limited temperature range with a quartz spring balance.
texts.text: The detector sensitivity, which is a function of the gas or vapor sensed, was determined for C02 and H20. Helium with 0.21 vol % C02 and nitrogen with 0.20 vol % H2, were used as test gases. Moreover, the permeation rates of C02, 02, and N2 through a PTFE membrane were measured in a Barrer-type permeation cell (Linde Model CS 135). and compared with the equivalent signals obtained in the PPA. The permeation coefficients for C02 derived by the two independent approaches differed by 7%; the data reported here are based on the calibration of the PPA. The mole response factors of the detector for 02 and N2 (relative to C02) were found to be 1.20 and 1.22; the factor for N02 was assumed to be unity because its molecular weight differs by only two units from that of C02. No N204 reached the detector since in the helium stream the highest partial pressure of the nitrogen oxides, expressed as N02, was less than 0.1 %. Finally, the equilibrium solubility of N204-N02 in PTFE at a total pressure of 1 atm was measured over a limited temperature range with a quartz spring balance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High purity C02, 02, and N2 were used without further purification or drying; the N204-N02 mixture was bled into the cell from a tank held at 30°. The PTFE used in this study had a density of 2.18 g/cc at 18° as determined by immersion in water. Membranes were cut with a microtome from a solid polymer block of Du Pont Teflon, commercial stock, and their thicknesses were determined by a micrometer with a reading precision of 0.05 mil. Only membranes with thickness variation of less than 5 % were used.
texts.text: High purity C02, 02, and N2 were used without further purification or drying; the N204-N02 mixture was bled into the cell from a tank held at 30°. The PTFE used in this study had a density of 2.18 g/cc at 18° as determined by immersion in water. Membranes were cut with a microtome from a solid polymer block of Du Pont Teflon, commercial stock, and their thicknesses were determined by a micrometer with a reading precision of 0.05 mil. Only membranes with thickness variation of less than 5 % were used.

texts.orig: A simplified method has been adopted for the evaluation of the large amount of data. The slope, which is given by the ratio (1 IX2)/At, was evaluated from two sets of two experimental points each, namely for S/Sa = 0.1, 0.4. and S/S' = 0.4, 0.7. For the noncondensable gases, the two values agreed within 5 % and were averaged.
texts.text: A simplified method has been adopted for the evaluation of the large amount of data. The slope, which is given by the ratio (1 IX2)/At, was evaluated from two sets of two experimental points each, namely for S/Sa = 0.1, 0.4. and S/S' = 0.4, 0.7. For the noncondensable gases, the two values agreed within 5 % and were averaged.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the N02-N204 system the determination of diffusion coefficients by the described formalism becomes questionable. The simultaneous diffusion of two interacting components must result in a complex rate law; unfortunately, the differential equation cannot be solved explicitly in this case. The experimental curves have shapes similar to those for the noncondensable gases (Figure 2a); however, plots of IjX2 vs. t (Figure 2b), though approximately linear, show a slight curvature and they intercept the time axes at negative values. Both effects diminish with increasing temperature, as would be expected from a consideration of the N204-N02 equilibrium. The nominal diffusion coefficients reported here were somewhat arbitrarily derived from the slopes for the interval S/Sco = 0.1,0.4.
texts.text: For the N02-N204 system the determination of diffusion coefficients by the described formalism becomes questionable. The simultaneous diffusion of two interacting components must result in a complex rate law; unfortunately, the differential equation cannot be solved explicitly in this case. The experimental curves have shapes similar to those for the noncondensable gases (Figure 2a); however, plots of IjX2 vs. t (Figure 2b), though approximately linear, show a slight curvature and they intercept the time axes at negative values. Both effects diminish with increasing temperature, as would be expected from a consideration of the N204-N02 equilibrium. The nominal diffusion coefficients reported here were somewhat arbitrarily derived from the slopes for the interval S/Sco = 0.1,0.4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The complete data cover a temperature range of 30-116°; however, the individual membranes were studied over smaller intervals, the range depending on the membrane thickness. Also, for any given membrane, permeation coefficients are usually given to higher temperatures than diffusion coefficients because the determination of the latter becomes unreliable for fast transient rates.
texts.text: The complete data cover a temperature range of 30-116°; however, the individual membranes were studied over smaller intervals, the range depending on the membrane thickness. Also, for any given membrane, permeation coefficients are usually given to higher temperatures than diffusion coefficients because the determination of the latter becomes unreliable for fast transient rates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the agreement between the data for different membranes was very good, they were all combined in a least square treatment. The straight lines in the figures represent the results. The mean square root deviation of the individual data points from the best line is between 3 and 5 % for all plots. Deviations for an individual membrane do not appear to be systematic or significant, and are well within the range of uncertainties originating from thickness measurement and slight variation in flow rate of the carrier gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The diffusion and permeation coefficients of C02, 02, and N2, plotted in Figures 3 and 4, confirm convincingly that the generally assumed simple diffusion law holds; the independence of the coefficients on membrane thickness, in particular, proves that the sorption process on the membrane surface is fast and is not the rate-determining step. The data also show that the activation energies of diffusion and permeation are independent of temperature, over the rather wide temperature range of this study.
texts.text: The diffusion and permeation coefficients of C02, 02, and N2, plotted in Figures 3 and 4, confirm convincingly that the generally assumed simple diffusion law holds; the independence of the coefficients on membrane thickness, in particular, proves that the sorption process on the membrane surface is fast and is not the rate-determining step. The data also show that the activation energies of diffusion and permeation are independent of temperature, over the rather wide temperature range of this study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The activation energies of diffusion Ed of the three gases (Table I) are quite similar. However, in view of the high precision of the data, the differences, though small, are significant. This is also indicated by the trend in the preexponential factors; their logarithms, i.e., the entropies of activation, are approximately a linear function of the activation energies of diffusion, as has been found in other systems.1 The trend in Ed appears to be associated with the molecular sizes of the gases. texts.orig: The heats of solution of the three gases in PTFE and their heats of condensation (last column) are compared in Table II. The close numerical agreement is probably fortuitous; nevertheless it indicates that solution in PTFE is similar to a condensation process with minimal interaction between the polymer and the solute.
texts.text: The heats of solution of the three gases in PTFE and their heats of condensation (last column) are compared in Table II. The close numerical agreement is probably fortuitous; nevertheless it indicates that solution in PTFE is similar to a condensation process with minimal interaction between the polymer and the solute.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A comparison of diffusion and solubility data of PTFE with those of the structurally similar linear polyethylene is of some interest (Table II). The polyethylene data are for a Phillips type 22% amorphous polymer and are taken from two papers by Michaels and Bixler;5 they are converted to the units used here. The solubilities refer to the amorphous phase and are calculated assuming the solubility in the crystalline phase to be negligible. The PTFE used was 40% amorphous as determined from its density.
texts.text: A comparison of diffusion and solubility data of PTFE with those of the structurally similar linear polyethylene is of some interest (Table II). The polyethylene data are for a Phillips type 22% amorphous polymer and are taken from two papers by Michaels and Bixler;5 they are converted to the units used here. The solubilities refer to the amorphous phase and are calculated assuming the solubility in the crystalline phase to be negligible. The PTFE used was 40% amorphous as determined from its density.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The activation energy of diffusion of the three gases in PTFE is about 2 kcal/mol lower than in polyethylene. This is surprising, since PTFE has very stiff backbone chains of high viscosity. Apparently, as proposed originally by Brandt and Anysas,1 diffusion of small molecules in this polymer proceeds through preformed channels and cavities and requires little movement of the polymer chains.
texts.text: The activation energy of diffusion of the three gases in PTFE is about 2 kcal/mol lower than in polyethylene. This is surprising, since PTFE has very stiff backbone chains of high viscosity. Apparently, as proposed originally by Brandt and Anysas,1 diffusion of small molecules in this polymer proceeds through preformed channels and cavities and requires little movement of the polymer chains.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The higher gas solubilities at 25°, more than six times those in polyethylene, and the higher heats of solution may again indicate poor packing of the PTFE chains in the amorphous regions. A part of the difference, however, may be due to the difference in cohesive energy density of the two polymers. The solubilities of N2, Oand COin perfluoroheptane, for example, are larger by a factor of 2-3 than in heptane, 6 Further study is required to establish the relative significance of the two effects.

texts.text: Figure 5. Permeation and diffusion coefficients of N-04NO- equilibrium mixtures in PTFE: (1) nominal diffusion coefficients of mixture; (2) nominal permeation coefficients of mixture; (3) permeation coefficients of N02; (4) permeation coefficients of N204.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2. Nitrogen Dioxide. Figure 5 summarizes the data for the N204-N02 system obtained with the same five membranes as for C02. Repeat runs were reproducible even after long time exposure of the membranes to the strong oxidant. Moreover, data for C02 obtained before and after exposure to N02 were identical within the precision of the measurements, evidence that PTFE is not attacked by NO-. The nominal diffusion coefficients (see preceding section) lie on a straight line (curve 1) except at the lower temperatures. The steady-state permeation data closely fit the theoretical curve 2 (see below), except for some points for the 4.3-mil membrane at lower temperatures.
texts.text: 2. Nitrogen Dioxide. Figure 5 summarizes the data for the N204-N02 system obtained with the same five membranes as for C02. Repeat runs were reproducible even after long time exposure of the membranes to the strong oxidant. Moreover, data for C02 obtained before and after exposure to N02 were identical within the precision of the measurements, evidence that PTFE is not attacked by NO-. The nominal diffusion coefficients (see preceding section) lie on a straight line (curve 1) except at the lower temperatures. The steady-state permeation data closely fit the theoretical curve 2 (see below), except for some points for the 4.3-mil membrane at lower temperatures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The close agreement of the apparent diffusion coefficients and the normalized permeation rates for different membrane thicknesses, shown in Figure 5, indicate again that the adsorption process is fast, and that the transport within the membrane is the ratedetermining step. This transport mechanism however is complex not only because two species diffuse, but also because the relative concentrations of N204 and ND2 in the gas phase and in the polymer are functions of temperature and total pressure (or concentration).

The diffusion parameters derived from it are ascribed to N02 and listed in Table IV.

texts.orig: It can be easily seen that the permeation data plotted in Figure 5 are in the form of this normalized flux Fo/ they can be analyzed in terms of eq 8. We postulate that both Ps ! and Ps,o4 show an exponential dependence on the reciprocal temperature. Since at the highest temperatures of the experiments the gas phase is virtually pure NO>, the permeation coefficients Pso2 are given by the asymptote to the high-temperature data, curve 3 in Figure 5, and can be extrapolated to lower temperatures. Moreover, the partial pressures of pso, are given by equilibrium eq 1. Consequently, only the permeation coefficient PK2o4 of eq 8 is unknown and can be calculated at different temperatures from the smoothed, experimental F0 values. After minor adjustments in the slope of the PNo, curve, a linear Fn-o, curve was obtained [(4) in Figure 5], Then, as a check, the total flux F0 was recalculated from the two permeation curves (3) and (4). The agreement of the calculated curve (2) with the experimental data is excellent, except for some points derived from the 4.3-mil membrane.
texts.text: It can be easily seen that the permeation data plotted in Figure 5 are in the form of this normalized flux Fo/ they can be analyzed in terms of eq 8. We postulate that both Ps ! and Ps,o4 show an exponential dependence on the reciprocal temperature. Since at the highest temperatures of the experiments the gas phase is virtually pure NO>, the permeation coefficients Pso2 are given by the asymptote to the high-temperature data, curve 3 in Figure 5, and can be extrapolated to lower temperatures. Moreover, the partial pressures of pso, are given by equilibrium eq 1. Consequently, only the permeation coefficient PK2o4 of eq 8 is unknown and can be calculated at different temperatures from the smoothed, experimental F0 values. After minor adjustments in the slope of the PNo, curve, a linear Fn-o, curve was obtained [(4) in Figure 5], Then, as a check, the total flux F0 was recalculated from the two permeation curves (3) and (4). The agreement of the calculated curve (2) with the experimental data is excellent, except for some points derived from the 4.3-mil membrane.

texts.orig: The parameters derived from the two permeation curves (3) and (4) are listed in Table IV. The coefficients, which in Figure 5 are given in atmospheric pressure units, are converted to centimeters to facilitate comparison with the C02, 02, and N2 data. Approximate solubility and diffusion coefficients of N204 at 25° are given also. The solubility coefficient was derived from the directly measured solubilities (Table II) by extrapolation to 25°. A correction was made for the solubility of the NO> present in the gas phase at 25°.
texts.text: The parameters derived from the two permeation curves (3) and (4) are listed in Table IV. The coefficients, which in Figure 5 are given in atmospheric pressure units, are converted to centimeters to facilitate comparison with the C02, 02, and N2 data. Approximate solubility and diffusion coefficients of N204 at 25° are given also. The solubility coefficient was derived from the directly measured solubilities (Table II) by extrapolation to 25°. A correction was made for the solubility of the NO> present in the gas phase at 25°.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A comparison of the solubility and diffusion coefficients of N204 and of C02, 02, N2 indicates a common pattern. The solubility coefficients of the gases correlate quite well with their boiling points, as has been found for other gas-polymer systems.8 Similarly, the reduction in the N204 diffusion coefficient by a factor of about 4 compared to C02 appears to be reasonable in view of the much larger size of the N204 molecule. Thus, N204, like the other gases, apparently does not interact with PTFE.
texts.text: A comparison of the solubility and diffusion coefficients of N204 and of C02, 02, N2 indicates a common pattern. The solubility coefficients of the gases correlate quite well with their boiling points, as has been found for other gas-polymer systems.8 Similarly, the reduction in the N204 diffusion coefficient by a factor of about 4 compared to C02 appears to be reasonable in view of the much larger size of the N204 molecule. Thus, N204, like the other gases, apparently does not interact with PTFE.

texts.orig: at all. Although the N02 molecule has approximately the same size as C02, its activation energy of diffusion is about twice and its heat of solution about three times that of C02. Also, its diffusion and solubility coefficients at 25° are smaller and larger, respectively, by orders of magnitude. These observations suggest that the highly polar N02 molecule interacts strongly with PTFE.
texts.text: at all. Although the N02 molecule has approximately the same size as C02, its activation energy of diffusion is about twice and its heat of solution about three times that of C02. Also, its diffusion and solubility coefficients at 25° are smaller and larger, respectively, by orders of magnitude. These observations suggest that the highly polar N02 molecule interacts strongly with PTFE.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A systematic analysis of the data in terms of molecular dimensions and interchain forces is not only of considerable interest, but is essential for a more detailed and quantitative understanding of diffusion mechanisms. This analysis must however be postponed until data for a larger number of permeants and PTFE samples of different degree of crystallinity are available.
texts.text: A systematic analysis of the data in terms of molecular dimensions and interchain forces is not only of considerable interest, but is essential for a more detailed and quantitative understanding of diffusion mechanisms. This analysis must however be postponed until data for a larger number of permeants and PTFE samples of different degree of crystallinity are available.

texts.orig: is still valid, and to incorporate the nongaussian behavior by substituting for (rv2 *) a form which adequately represents the actual mean quadratic separation between segments. This ad hoc procedure has been extensively used to study the influence on P of excluded volume,3-5 chain stiffness,5,6 and the detailed structure of the chain7 (represented by the rotational isomeric model). It is, thus, of great interest to estimate the error that is committed by these type of calculations which apply the gaussian form (2) to nongaussian model chains. A step in this direction is the work of Nagai, who showed8 that, for a generic distribution, (sin / ,'/ /·,·;) can be expanded in a power series whose zeroth order term is the gaussian exponential exp( -2· (rti2>/6).

texts.orig: The purpose of this note is to determine, numerically, the error committed with the gaussian approximation (2) in the case of two very simple nongaussian models, for which a closed expression of the exact P is known. The models are the conditional probability chain (CP) presented by Verstratte and Frankenberg,9 and the zigzag chain (ZZ) or broken rod.10
